reasoning: As of mid-May 2025, the major AI copyright and training data lawsuits (including NYT v. OpenAI, UMG v. Anthropic, Doe v. GitHub, and various class actions) are either bogged down in protracted discovery disputes, dealing with interlocutory appeals, or have just recently survived motions to dismiss. None have a trial date set that could reasonably lead to a final judgment by December 31, 2025. Complex, high-stakes US litigation typically takes several years to reach a trial, let alone a final enforceable order. Furthermore, the companies have existential incentives to fight these claims on "fair use" grounds rather than accepting a swift $>100M judgment. While a massive early settlement is theoretically possible, courts would have to approve it as a formal order, and the timeline for class-action settlement approval procedures alone usually spans 6-12 months. Given the absence of any newly announced settlements or massive FTC/DOJ fines under the current administration, a $>100M court order before the end of 2025 is exceedingly unlikely.
